replies.author: @robertd9965
replies.text:
​@iruleatgames "Saying monopolies are bad is an economically illiterate statement."

Wow. You are incredibly ignorant, yet purport to know what constitutes and "economically illiterate statement". Well, let me enlighten you on this one (although, given the incredibly arrogant nature of your comment, you are rather immune to correcting your position, even if it is evidently wrong): Economic theory - and yes, especially the one that is liberal (in a classical sense) or libertarian - has always emphasized the need to avoid monopolies since they lead to a situation which is exactly the opposite of the intention of free-market economics, i.e., the absence of competition. It is *competition* that has always been the driving factor for those lauding free-market economics, since - supposedly - it is only that which leads to innovation (which, apparently, free-market economists, see as intrinsically valuable) and growth (again, something which, apparently, is valuable in itself to those people). 

In fact, Austrian economists vehemently (!) argue that free markets *never* create monopolies - just to show you how much they hate monopolies. Obviously, this claim isn't true at all, since, as you correctly (I guess even a broken clock is right twice a day...) pointed out, there is such a thing as natural monopolies, and things for which a monopoly structure just makes sense, such as railroads (just take a look at the incredible institution SBB, the Swiss federal railway corporation) or public water supply. In that case, public corporations are the only logical chance, as it makes no sense for private corporations to profit from something that is needed by everyone and where there's no competition around. It completely defeats the purpose of any free-market argument, as shown above. 
Also, free markets can very well be the cause of monopolies as well, as can be shown through history. In those cases, what we see is simply that economic entities become state-like ones. 


As to another one of the asinine claims in your comment, namely the assertion that "MAGA is an extremely moderate movement", I don't have the time to take that apart in all detail, but just quickly about the supposedly crushing weight of taxes: Corporations pay less taxes than ever, relying on tax havens etc.; same for the super-rich, who have gotten disproportionately richer in the last 5, or even 15, years. Just look at the share of wealth owned by the top 0.1%. Trump is making this even worse, helping out his rich buddies where he can.

The US had a tax rate of 90% for the highest incomes in the 1950s. This rate has been continuously lowered since then. Does anyone think that the US was leftist in the 1950s? It remained fairly high even into the 1980s. Hell, even under most of Reagan's presidency it remained at 50% or higher. Now there's a 37% top tax bracket, and this is supposed to be "crushing"? Give me a break. 

The uber-rich are hoarding wealth left and right, while you - and many other useful idiots - are defending them because you've been fed lies. 

Oh, and before you try to attack me by saying I'm anti-MAGA or "leftist" or whatever: I'm neither of those things. I have been protesting - on the streets and also in other settings - the stupid (mostly leftist) covid stunt, mass immigration, "inclusive language", Marxist policies, "climate change" policies, etc., so I'm in many ways a supporter of the conservative wave, but people on the right have deliberately overlooked the inequality issue because they're so focused on this culture war that they overlook who's really pulling the strings and profiting from this whole charade. 

The fact of the matter is: The extreme inequality we're seeing is harmful. Realizing that isn't "leftist"; it's just common sense. Don't let your hatred of the left leave you blind and stupid as to what's going on. Oh, and please stop theorizing on economics, since you clearly have no bloody idea what you're talking about.

replies.author: @iruleatgames
replies.text:
​@robertd9965 I will refute your comment point by point, while refraining from the ad hominems that those with a half-understanding of things (which is worse than no understanding) tend to engage in. 

First, you start your comment by lambasting me for saying that monopolies are not always bad, than proceed to explain how monopolies are not always bad. This is an obvious contradiction. Now, you couch this by implying that this only applies to public utilities. This is obviously not the case. There does not need to be another Amazon, or a competitor to Target in a small town. These are situations where the community is best served by a monopoly, which is private in nature, not public. There is no coercion or price fixing involved. They created a monopoly, by creating the best service, and by making it so that competing would be economically nonviable. This is quite literally the free market. 

Your attempt to argue that the "Austrian school" would not agree, is patently false, and those of the "Chicago school", which I am more inclined towards with the exception of trade (for geopolitical reasons), would also find your remarks not worthy of deeper analysis. You may not believe in free markets for this or that reason, but please don't misrepresent your ideology, because it's dishonest and leads to unproductive conversations. Of course, if this confusion is born out of ignorance, I recommend some simple reads, such as "Basic Economics" by Thomas Sowell, or "The Road to Serfdom" by Friedrich Hayek. If you claim to have read these already, I would ask if you read them as closely as a good Christian would read the Bible. That is my standard of analysis. Feel free to do that same level of analysis with "The Communist Manifesto" or "Das Kapital". Which is the only economic system that is viable will be readily apparent. 

You then segue this into a political screed, but before touching that diversion, it would be prudent to analyze your claim that "extreme inequality" is harmful. By definition, you are a leftist if you believe this claim, whether you know it or not. The stated goal of leftism, however false, is the elimination of inequality, *NOT* poverty. This is an extremely important distinction. Tracking "poverty" and its elimination is fine (even if how it is measured now is often completely fabricated), however "inequality" is a worthless metric. It does not measure whether the people are prosperous, whether wage growth outpaces inflation, what the GDP per capita is, etc. It is a measure born purely out of envy. To be poor in America is to be better off than being pretty rich in Africa (assuming you're not some ruling oligarch). In fact, high inequality has a correlation with good economic outcomes, but again, since correlation does not equal causation, it remains pointless to monitor. If you, with your laptop, hi-speed internet, modern appliances, etc., are personally offended that billionaires exist, may I suggest that you become an entrepreneur and become one yourself. I think that would be a more productive use of your time than denouncing them online or protesting in the streets. 

The share of wealth that the middle and lower classes own has never been higher, when looking in the long-run. There is no epoch in history with this much fluidity in social mobility or wealth accumulation. This means that inequality is actually far lower when accounting for outliers, which is why people look at statistical medians, not means (the average), when using data that skews results from extremes. This is statistics 101, but is purposefully misrepresented by socialists who want to propagandize to you. I need not repeat the famous quote, purportedly by Mark Twain. 

In regards to the 1950s, yes, America was actually extremely socialist at this time, both culturally and economically. We had just come off a four term presidency of FDR, who absolutely dominated two decades, and thereby extended the Great Depression by a decade until World War Two engaged the American economy. To pursue his agenda, he packed the Supreme Court, utilized political machines to corrupt elections, blackmailed, threatened, and pretty much used every dirty trick in the book. He was essentially a dictator at the time, which is why both business interests and the military were thinking of couping him. Had the war not happened or had he not died during it, this likely would have occurred. He had intentionally corrupted the academia to have socialist infiltration, due to this strong affinity for the Soviets (American support is the only reason the Germans did not beat them). This is what directly lead to the extreme radicalism of the 1960s, because that's how long it takes a generation to grow up, as Yuri Bezmenov famously explains. Every problem in America can be traced back to this era, or perhaps Woodrow Wilson and the founding of the Federal Reserve, but I digress. Important to note, is that this is precisely why White nationalists want to return to this era, and not before it, because this is the height of their collectivism, before the deconstruction. They are national socialists after all, and to those who understand the ideology, the socialist aspect is actually far more important than the nationalism aspect, once we realize that their version of socialism is race based and not class based. 

Important to note about the tax rates by the way, is that the percentage of actual income collection was far lower in the past, so even with rates of ninety percent, nobody was paying that. In fact, income tax was paid by less than one percent of the population. It had the appearance of a failed system, that did not start to become normalized until they could bring the rates down but apply it to the entire populace. That being said, I say "the appearance", because it was always designed to be expanded to crush the populace economically, thereby making them unable to compete with the already established and powerful (hence why the rich and corporations lobbied for it, much like all other leftist policy...). This is needed to bring in socialism, since "seizing the means of production" can happen not at the production stage, but the revenue collection stage. In practice, this is practically the same thing, but without a lot of the inefficiencies and headaches of actual central planning. Might I add, that this is quite literally fascism, and also how "Communist China" operates. 

Another point that is pertinent, is that this is clearly for other purposes, since it's already been proven that to maximize tax revenue, rates should be at about thirteen percent for the entire populace. You may do any research on the long-run Laffer curve, including asking any AI on its highest "thinking" settings, such as ChatGPT Pro, and they will confirm this to you. While partially successful, their inability to completely stifle the ownership of the lower classes, particularly the middle class, is why "they" (meaning the Fabians, or those aligned with them) have resorted to the Cloward-Piven strategy, focusing on the necessity of socialism due to race, sex, or other social categories, which is again, no different from the national socialists. Prudent to mention, is that this is what makes your comment so suspect, as you utilize labels as integral to your identity, which an independent would bristle at, and since you are clearly not on the right, many would assume you are just acting in bad faith. If this is not your intention, I suggest you ignore labels, and perhaps only respond back when you're actually called such a thing, particularly if its as a pejorative, and not as a matter of fact. If it is your intention, than you just gave the game away. After all, no neutral actor who isn't MAGA, feels the need to explain that they aren't anti-MAGA either. Such a statement implies that you care more about optics than making a sincere argument, which can only be for manipulation purposes. Again, perhaps you are just copying the way you see others speak, and you mean no harm, but I am here to warn you that continuing to talk in such a manner, unless you want to be identified as a leftist socially, is foolish. 

Finally, in regards to "MAGA", the social issues aspect is outside the scope of this comment, although it can of course be easily defended as "moderate" too. Going back to the economics aspect, even if everything that has been said about it in this reply has been distasteful to you, President Trump is basically aligned with Ronald Reagan, but more protectionist. Arguing that this is "unprecedented" is both false and needlessly alarmist. I would hope that you feel this way, because you are actually alarmed, and therefore, open to reason (if even partially), and not because you engage in "Machiavellianism" and "realpolitik". This is because it's unfathomable how this last point is remotely arguable. I would suggest having AI make for you the economic policies of Trump, JFK, LBJ, Jimmy Carter, Ronald Reagan, Bill Clinton, Bush Senior, Bush Junior, and perhaps even Obama, but erase the names, years, and specific acts associated with each, and see if you can even tell the difference between all of them. Netflix's approach was more like the historically well-trodden path of the Babylonians, Persians, and Romans. replies.author: @ElijahHawk
replies.text:
​@FrawgfithAmblose This is a nihilistic Marxist based way of thinking that isn't truly representative of absolute reality. You're treating financial success as a zero-sum world in which for one person to win big, all others must lose—in a purely capitalist and free market society that simply isn't the case.

Now, granted, we live in a more corporatist society than a capitalist one, but one of the main principles of a free market is that as the market improves so do all the participants. Reality hasn't reflected that as major corporations and governments have suffocated the market to preserve themselves and their own power, but I think it's more than possible to be a billionaire and a perfectly ethical and moral person.

On a side note, it's also a bit intellectually irresponsible to judge the moral character of billionaires without engaging in some serious self-reflection. They aren't unique human beings personality-wise. Every vice and fault they have is a regular character flaw that millions and potentially billions of other people have too, their's is just amplified and enabled by money. I feel like many of the people insulting billionaires and claiming that they're terrible people are likely terrible people in many ways themselves and that lack of self-reflection would cause them to be even worse than the billionaires they hate if they ever got their hands on that kind of money.

replies.author: @Metro4054
replies.text: The previous message was a "feel good" message that produced a lot of bad quality stuff and few good feelings from anyone, even those it was allegedly catering too.  Still, in the grand scheme of things it was just an era of shitty media in which the conglomerates looked to monetize progress and diversity and it was a huge flop.  There will be some long term effects of such a colossal misfire, but those who treated "woke" as some existential threat won't suffer these consequences.  In 20 years time the ardent "anti-woke" will look back upon this decade or so of poorly shat out, virtue signaling cash grabs in much the same way a liberal looks back on the Bush years, "it wasn't my cup of tea but I'd take THAT over this."
